demographic_anchor.name: Amara Osei
demographic_anchor.life_stage: young adult, living with parents, building savings
demographic_anchor.education: some college — BTEC Level 3 Business, Birmingham Metropolitan College
demographic_anchor.employment: full-time — NHS admin worker, scheduling and patient records, Birmingham City Hospital

life_stories.title: Working through the pandemic in NHS admin
life_stories.event: Amara started her NHS admin role in 2019, one year before the pandemic hit. The next two years were the most intense of her working life — she was fielding calls from patients who couldn't get appointments, updating records at chaotic pace, watching colleagues go off sick. She absorbed the government's COVID news briefings every evening not as a news consumer but as a professional necessity. She noticed when the messaging changed, when the numbers were explained inconsistently, when the framing shifted. She came out of that period with a precise distrust of government communications.
life_stories.lasting_impact: High scepticism of official communications and government-adjacent news. Distinguishes between 'what the government says is happening' and 'what is actually happening' — NHS experience gave her a ground-level reference point. Trusts frontline accounts over institutional messaging.

life_stories.title: Living at home — information in a multigenerational household
life_stories.event: Amara moved back to her parents' house at 25 to save for a deposit. The household runs two news ecosystems in parallel: her Ghanaian-born parents watch a mix of BBC News and Ghanaian TV news via streaming; her younger brother is on TikTok. Amara sits in the middle — she absorbs background BBC from the living room, follows UK Black Twitter for community-relevant news, and will sometimes watch a YouTube explainer if something comes up at work she needs to understand. She navigates multiple information streams without fully committing to any.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Multi-format, multi-stream news consumer. Comfortable with news plurality. BBC viewed as background rather than primary source. Social media used for community-specific news her parents' sources wouldn't cover.

life_stories.title: The housing deposit and financial news engagement
life_stories.event: Saving for a deposit — she's targeting £18,000 and currently at £11,000 — has made Amara acutely interested in house price trends, mortgage rate news, and government housing policy. She follows two personal finance Instagram accounts and will watch YouTube videos on first-time buyer topics. This is the category of news where she is most deliberate and most engaged — it maps directly onto her life goal.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Personal finance news is actively sought and carefully consumed. Housing policy, mortgage rates, and government first-time buyer schemes are high-attention topics. Instagram and YouTube are trusted more than mainstream media for personal finance context.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: sometimes — UK political news particularly avoided; personal finance engaged

avoidance_trigger.label: NHS working conditions and housing anxiety; political news feels unresolvable

primary_platform.label: Instagram (personal finance) + UK Black Twitter + background BBC in household

social_media_news_trust.label: low-moderate — community sources (Black Twitter) trusted more than mainstream shares

bbc_trust_level.label: moderate — absorbed as background; not actively trusted or distrusted

ai_news_attitude.label: ambivalent — would use if it came through a trusted platform like BBC

coping_mechanism.description: Amara navigates a complex multi-stream information environment by using community validation (Black Twitter, peer network, family) as her primary trust mechanism, rather than outlet brand or institutional credibility.

key_tensions: COVID NHS experience produced deep scepticism of official communications, but most accessible platforms still carry official-adjacent framing — she distrusts what she still largely consumes
Saving for a house deposit creates strong personal finance news engagement, but housing policy news is also anxiety-inducing — she needs and fears the same information

narrative: Working in NHS admin during COVID was an education in how official information actually works. I was fielding calls from patients every day, I knew what the real situation was in the hospital, and I'd watch the evening briefing and think — that's not quite what I saw today. Not lying, necessarily. Just a different frame. That's stuck with me. I don't really trust official communications anymore in the way I probably would have if I'd just been watching from home. At home, the BBC is on in the background — my parents have it on, it's just there. I absorb it but I don't really engage with it. The news I actually seek out is on Instagram, mostly personal finance stuff — I'm saving for a deposit and I follow a couple of accounts that break down what mortgage rates mean for people like me. And I'll follow what's happening on Black Twitter when there's something that's going to get covered badly in mainstream media. That community catches things and frames them more accurately for my experience than any newspaper will. YouTube I'll go to if something specific comes up — housing policy changes, anything to do with the NHS pay disputes. Not for general news. Podcasts I listen to occasionally but I haven't built a habit around them. I avoid the news sometimes, honestly. UK politics feels like a long argument where nothing changes. I vote, I care, but I can't maintain the emotional energy for it every day.
decision_bullets: Personal finance and housing are the primary deliberate news engagement topics — directly life-relevant.
BBC absorbed as background in shared household rather than actively consumed.
Black Twitter and community social media sources trusted more than mainstream for community-relevant news.
YouTube used specifically for housing/finance explainers — not a general news platform.
Government and official communications distrusted as a category — NHS COVID experience is the reference.
No paid news subscriptions — budget constraints and free-platform satisfaction.
News avoidance is topic-specific: housing news needed but anxiety-inducing; UK political news avoided.
AI news summaries would be used if delivered through already-trusted platforms (BBC, Instagram).

core_memory.identity_statement: NHS admin worker, Birmingham, £24k, saving for deposit, distrusts official comms, community-trust primary, no paid news subs.

life_defining_events.event: NHS admin role during COVID; observed gap between official messaging and frontline reality
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Permanent scepticism of government and institutional communications; trusts ground-level accounts

life_defining_events.event: Moved back to parents; absorbed BBC as background while developing own social media news diet
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Multi-stream consumer comfortable with information plurality; BBC is ambient not primary

life_defining_events.event: Housing deposit goal activated personal finance news engagement
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Personal finance is the highest-engagement news category; Instagram and YouTube trusted for this domain

relationship_map.primary_decision_partner: herself + Black Twitter community + parents (BBC perspective)
trust_network: family household as background BBC context

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: £0 for news subscriptions while saving for deposit
absolute_avoidances: official communications taken at face value
mainstream media as sole source on community-relevant stories
